changes.text: A meadow, a river, and trees good for food take shape.
The Tree of Life and the Tree of Knowledge stand at the garden's heart.
Birds, a lamb, and drifting light fill it with quiet life.
Adam — or Eve — can walk the garden for the first time.
Eve joins Adam in the garden; the other always wanders nearby.
A word of welcome now greets every visitor at the gate.
The river parts east of the garden into four heads, fanning through the mist.
Five fish — one gold — swim and rise in the shallows.
A whole day now passes in seven minutes, morning to starlit night.
The moon takes the light after the sun sets.
Four standing stones name the river's heads: Pishon, Gihon, Tigris, Euphrates.
The garden finds its voice: wind, water, birdsong, and crickets, woven live.
Doves and cattle graze and roost beside the lamb.
A morning mist rises to water the ground.
The night sky bears its signs: the Bear, Orion, and the Pleiades.
Drawing near the Tree of Life kindles a quiet reverence.
Sound becomes opt-in, and the garden's voice is gentled.
Draw near any creature and its Hebrew name is given — Taleh the lamb, Yonah the dove, and more.
Clouds cast real shade, and a gentle rain sometimes passes over.
The night sky wheels slowly through the seasons of a long year.
Butterflies and bees fill the flowers, each named as they're found.

log.focus: stillness, the banks, and the east

changes.text: Stand still by the water and the shyer creatures draw near.
Reeds grow soft along the river's banks.
A gate of pale stone stands far to the east, a hush of what will keep the way.
Something unnamed stirs the grass near the Tree of Knowledge, and is gone.
The river takes on the hour's colour, and the banks reflect in it.
Every step through the water leaves a widening ring.
Petals drift from the trees and settle into the grass.
Dew gathers overnight and glints at first light.
Two sweeps of light gather above the eastern gate at dawn — the keeper, still hinted, never shown.
At evening a wind moves through the garden, and the trees bow as it passes.
West of the garden, the river is found rising among wet stones.
Figs and pomegranates ripen on a few trees, named as you draw near.
The lamb finds two companions, and the cattle low softly at dusk.
Footprints linger a while in the sandy banks, then fade.
Fallen fig leaves lie sewn together at the foot of the dark tree.
Gold, bdellium, and onyx glint among the stones where the Pishon runs.
The birds nest in the sacred trees' branches, and in time a chick or two of their own.
On the seventh day the garden keeps a deeper rest — even the wind at peace.
The Tree of Life bears its own fruit in season, not just gold leaves.
At first light the morning stars sing together, just before the day quiets them.
A tree stands apart at the river's bank, its leaf never withering, fruited every season.
The valleys fill with grain, swaying together the moment the evening wind walks through.
Goats keep the rising rim, sure-footed among the high stones.
An eagle circles high above the garden, and makes her nest higher still.
A hart keeps the riverside, panting after the water brooks.

log.focus: after the rain, and the small and slow

changes.text: The rain leaves the sky clear and blue behind it, cloud by cloud, once its hour has passed.
Sit still by the water a while, and the nearest lamb comes to lie down beside it.
A stork keeps her nest high in her own fir tree, above every other nest in the garden.
Locusts go forth by bands across the meadow, no king leading any of them.
A creeping thing keeps close to the ground by the water's edge, slowest of all.

next: After the shower, low puddles glisten a while in the ground before the earth drinks them in (Psalm 65:10)
Wildflowers dot the meadow grass in quick dabs of color among the green (Genesis 1:11)
A wild ass ranges far and free over the plain, keeping its own distance from every path (Job 39:5-8)
The hart's fawn keeps close beside her, unsteady but quick to follow (Job 39:1)
